content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
  
 
March 26, 2020 
 
 
Joanna Gubman 
1825 Vine St #1, 
Berkeley, CA 94703 
 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
 
Our File No.  I-20-041 
 
Dear Ms. Gubman: 
 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the “revolving door” provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).1 Because your inquiry is general in nature, we are treating 
your request as one for informal assistance.2 
 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
 
QUESTIONS 
 
1. 
Under the Act’s permanent ban and as a former state official, may you work for an outside 
employer in bidding on an energy program contract, given that you previously participated in the 
California Public Utilities Commission’s (“CPUC”) authorization and/or funding of the program, 
which included certain program directions and requirements? 
 
2. 
Under the same circumstances, may you work for an outside employer in implementing a 
contract the employer has already been awarded? 
 
 
 
 
 
 
1  The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2  Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 


File No. I-20-041 
Page No. 2 
 
 
CONCLUSIONS 
 
1. 
No, where the CPUC’s authorization and/or funding of a program was accompanied by 
program requirements and directions that would foreseeably shape the program administrators’ 
requests for proposals and bid selections, the authorization/funding constitutes a part of the same 
proceeding as the awarding of the program contract, and additional participation for an outside 
employer is prohibited under the Act. 
 
2. 
Yes, because the formation and awarding of a contract is considered a separate proceeding 
from the implementation of a contract under the Act, the permanent ban does not apply provided 
you did not work on the implementation of the contract subsequent to the award of the contract and 
prior to leaving state service. 
 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
 
 
You left the CPUC in January 2019. While at the CPUC, you worked as an advisor to a 
Commissioner and were classified as a “decision-maker.” You previously worked in one 
proceeding in which the CPUC authorized funds for the investor-owned utilities and California 
Energy Commission (“CEC”) to operate programs for energy research and development (“R&D”). 
The CEC and utilities may do some in-house R&D, but most funds are bid out to third parties 
(universities, national labs, private companies, etc.). The other proceeding you were involved in 
authorized utility energy efficiency programs and required that some of those programs be bid out 
to third parties (private companies, non-profits, etc.). 
 
In both instances, the resultant programs are not administered by the CPUC. Instead, the 
programs are administered by the investor-owned utilities organizations or the CEC (“program 
administrators”). The CPUC gave general direction, such as that the overall portfolio of efficiency 
programs should be cost effective, that R&D programs should consider the needs of and aim to 
benefit disadvantaged communities, and that R&D programs should consider climate adaption 
needs. However, the CPUC did not set out contract terms or program details for specific programs. 
 
The program administrators develop the requests for proposals (“RFPs”) and evaluate and 
accept or reject bids. The CPUC does not oversee those aspects of program implementation. You do 
not know how bids are evaluated or selected, and those matters are in the hands of the program 
administrators, not the CPUC. However, after programs are operational, the CPUC conducts 
evaluations to ensure that its requirements (e.g., cost-effectiveness) were met. A document included 
on a website you directed us to3, “Regulating Energy Efficiency – A Primer on the CPUC’s Energy 
Efficiency Programs,” explains that all of the energy efficiency programs administered by investor-
owned utility (“IOU”) companies “are regulated by the CPUC to ensure they are meeting the goals 
and cost-effectiveness metrics the CPUC is statutorily required to set for the IOU efficiency 
portfolios.” Subsequently, CPUC staff members “assess how much energy these programs save and 
how much those savings cost. This information helps inform the CPUC’s Commissioners so they 
can determine how best to allocate California’s large, publicly funded energy efficiency budget.” 
 
 
 
3 https://www.cpuc.ca.gov/egyefficiency/ 


File No. I-20-041 
Page No. 3 
 
 
 
 
ANALYSIS 
 
 
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act, the “one-year ban” and the “permanent ban.” You are no 
longer subject to the one-year ban because you left state service in January 2019. 
 
The permanent ban on “switching sides” prohibits a former “state administrative official,”  
such as yourself, from working for compensation on certain proceedings in which the official 
participated while working for the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 18741.1.) An 
official is considered to have “participated” in a proceeding if he or she took part in the proceeding 
“personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written 
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential 
information as an officer or employee . . . .” (Section 87400(d).) “A supervisor is deemed to have 
participated in any proceeding that was ‘pending before,’ as defined in subdivision (b) of 
Regulation 18438.2, the official’s agency and that was under his or her supervisory authority.” 
(Regulation 18741.1(a)(4).) A proceeding is under a supervisor’s “supervisory authority” if any of 
the following applies to the supervisor: 
 
• The supervisor's duties include the primary responsibility within the agency for directing the 
operation or function of the program where the proceeding is initiated or conducted. 
However, this provision does not apply to a supervisor who is only responsible for the 
general oversight of the administrative actions or functions of a program in which the 
responsibilities concerning the specific or final review of the proceeding are expressly 
delegated to other persons in the agency. 
• The supervisor directly supervises the person performing the investigation, review, or other 
action involved in the proceeding including, but not limited to, assigning the matter for 
which the required conduct is taken. 
• The supervisor reviews, discusses, or authorizes any action in the proceeding. 
• The supervisor has contact with any of the participants in the proceeding regarding the 
subject of the proceeding. 
 
(Regulation 18741.1(a)(4).) 
 
The permanent ban applies to any formal or informal appearance or any oral or written 
communication—or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in representing any other 
person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or communication—made with the 
intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding in which you participated while 
you served as a state administrative official. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 18741.1.) 
“‘Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding’ means any proceeding, application, request for a 
ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest 
or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative 
agency . . . .” (Section 87400(c), italics added.) 
 
The permanent ban does not apply to a “new” proceeding even in cases where the new 
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the former state official had 


File No. I-20-041 
Page No. 4 
 
 
participated. (See Sections 87401, 87402.) The application, drafting, and awarding of a contract, 
license, or approval is considered a proceeding separate from the monitoring and performance or 
implementation of the contract, license, or approval. (See Hafer Advice Letter, I-18-202.) However, 
where a former official has already participated in the implementation of a contract, he or she may 
not work for compensation for an outside employer on the same implementation proceeding. (Yam 
Advice Letter, No. I-17-292; Culp Advice Letter, No. I-14-051.) Likewise, where a former official 
has already taken part in the formation of a contract, license, or approval, the official may not work 
for compensation for an outside employer prior to the awarding of the contract, as that constitutes 
the same proceeding. 
 
With respect to companies that have not yet been awarded a program contract, you have 
explained that although the CPUC is the entity that authorized or provided funding for certain 
energy efficiency programs, it is the program administrators, and not the CPUC, that develop the 
RFPs and evaluate and selects bids. However, you have also explained that after programs are 
operational, the CPUC conducts evaluations to ensure that its requirements (e.g., cost-effectiveness) 
were met. Presumably, then, the “general directions” that the CPUC gave when authorizing the 
programs or funding help shape the RFPs and how bids are evaluated. To that extent, the CPUC’s 
authorization and funding of the program, when conditioned on meeting certain requirements, 
constitutes a part of the formation of the eventual program contracts. In those instances, because the 
formation and awarding of a contract are considered parts of the same proceeding, you may not 
work on submitting an outside employer’s bid on a contract if you “previously participated” in its 
formation, as defined under the Act, including where you rendered advice on a substantial basis. 
(See Section 87400(d); Regulation 18741.1.) On the other hand, in instances where the CPUC’s 
authorization or funding of a program was not accompanied by any program requirements, 
participation in the CPUC’s decision would not be considered participation in the formation of a 
contract, and your subsequent work for an outside employer in submitting a bid would not be 
prohibited by the permanent ban. 
 
As discussed above, the permanent ban would not prohibit you from working to implement 
a contract that has already been awarded to your potential outside employer, as the implementation 
of that contract is considered a different proceeding than the formation and awarding of a contract, 
and thus is not prohibited by the permanent ban, provided you did not previously participate in the 
implementation of the contract subsequent to the award of the contract and prior to leaving state 
service.4 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
4 We also note that you have requested advice regarding the Public Contract Code, which the FPPC does not 
advise on. Accordingly, you may wish to consult with independent counsel in that regard. 


File No. I-20-041 
Page No. 5 
 
 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Sincerely,  
 
 
Dave Bainbridge 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
General Counsel  
 
 
 
Kevin Cornwall 
 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division 
KMC:aja

sections.analysis:
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act, the “one-year ban” and the “permanent ban.” You are no 
longer subject to the one-year ban because you left state service in January 2019. 
 
The permanent ban on “switching sides” prohibits a former “state administrative official,”

participated in any proceeding that was ‘pending before,’ as defined in subdivision (b) of

The permanent ban does not apply to a “new” proceeding even in cases where the new

participated. (See Sections 87401, 87402.) The application, drafting, and awarding of a contract,

4 We also note that you have requested advice regarding the Public Contract Code, which the FPPC does not 
advise on. Accordingly, you may wish to consult with independent counsel in that regard.

QUESTION: 1. 
Under the Act’s permanent ban and as a former state official, may you work for an outside 
employer in bidding on an energy program contract, given that you previously participated in the 
California Public Utilities Commission’s (“CPUC”) authorization and/or funding of the program, 
which included certain program directions and requirements? 
 
2. 
Under the same circumstances, may you work for an outside employer in implementing a 
contract the employer has already been awarded?2  Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

CONCLUSION: 1. 
No, where the CPUC’s authorization and/or funding of a program was accompanied by 
program requirements and directions that would foreseeably shape the program administrators’ 
requests for proposals and bid selections, the authorization/funding constitutes a part of the same 
proceeding as the awarding of the program contract, and additional participation for an outside 
employer is prohibited under the Act. 
 
2. 
Yes, because the formation and awarding of a contract is considered a separate proceeding 
from the implementation of a contract under the Act, the permanent ban does not apply provided 
you did not work on the implementation of the contract subsequent to the award of the contract and 
prior to leaving state service.
